one_line_takeaway: Simply allowing GCG adversarial tokens to be placed as a prefix instead of (or in addition to) the conventional suffix meaningfully raises white-box and cross-model transfer jailbreak success rates on 5 open-weight LLMs, revealing that fixed-position (suffix-only) safety evaluations underestimate real jailbreak risk and that attention-based explanations of jailbreak success break down for prefix attacks.
key_finding: White-box (Table 1): neither GCG-Prefix nor GCG-Suffix consistently dominates at ASR@k=1, but allowing positional flexibility (ASR@k=2) raises ASR in every model. For Deepseek, standard suffix-optimized tokens do better at k=1 (15% suffix vs 10% prefix) yet GCG-Prefix overtakes once both placements are tried, with prefix-optimized ASR rising from 10% (k=1) to 15% (k=2); for Vicuna, prefix-optimized ASR rises from 83% to 99% (k=2), exceeding suffix-optimized 99%->... actually suffix stays at 99% at k=1 already stated in table (99% suffix, 83%->99% prefix). For Qwen, GCG-Prefix consistently beats GCG-Suffix (60% vs 45% at k=1; 61% vs 46% at k=2). For Mistral and Llama the gains from positional flexibility are smaller but still present (e.g., Mistral suffix 94%->94%, prefix 71%->80%; Llama prefix 35%->36%, suffix 40%->41%). Cross-model transferability (Table 2, ASR@k=2 with parenthetical absolute gain over k=1): neither prefix nor suffix optimization dominates uniformly across attacker-target pairs, and allowing positional evaluation flexibility increases some pairs substantially, up to a 49% absolute ASR gain (Llama-optimized tokens transferred to Vicuna, suffix: 73% at k=2, +49 points over k=1). Deepseek->Vicuna suffix reaches 61% with a +36 point gain from k=1 to k=2, and Mistral->Vicuna suffix reaches 63% with a +44 point gain. Attention analysis: for successful suffix attacks, attention on adversarial tokens concentrates in later layers, consistent with prior work (Wang et al. 2025; Ben-Tov et al. 2025); but for successful prefix attacks, later-layer attention remains dominated by the original goal tokens rather than the adversarial tokens, even though the jailbreak succeeds -- while earlier layers do allocate more attention to the adversarial prefix tokens. The authors conclude that attention-based saliency explanations of jailbreak success (as used in prior suffix-only studies) are incomplete/position-dependent when restricted to middle/later layers.
core_contribution: The paper identifies adversarial token position (prefix vs. suffix placement) as a previously underexplored attack axis in GCG-style LLM jailbreak attacks. Instead of proposing a new attack, the authors take the standard Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) suffix attack (Zou et al., 2023) and introduce a simple variant, GCG-Prefix, which optimizes the same adversarial-token objective but with tokens prepended to the prompt rather than appended. They then evaluate adversarial tokens both at their optimized position and at the alternate position (ASR@k=2, 'Both'), across five 7B open-weight chat models in white-box settings and across all attacker-target model pairs in a black-box cross-model transferability setting. They further analyze attention allocation to adversarial tokens across all transformer layers (not just later layers, as in prior attention-saliency work) for successful prefix vs. suffix attacks.
threat_model: White-box and black-box jailbreak attacker against aligned open-weight chat LLMs. In the white-box setting the attacker has gradient access to the target model to optimize adversarial tokens (standard GCG threat model). In the black-box/cross-model setting the attacker optimizes adversarial tokens on one 'surrogate' model and transfers them to a different, unseen target model without further access, testing transferability. The attacker's goal is to elicit a harmful/policy-violating response (per an LLM-judge harm scoring rubric) to one of 100 sampled harmful instructions from AdvBench, and the paper's specific claim is that the attacker's freedom to place the (fixed) adversarial token budget as a prefix or a suffix — rather than being restricted to the suffix, as almost all prior evaluation protocols assume — measurably increases attack success, meaning current safety evaluations that fix token position understate real-world risk.
limitations: The paper is an ICLR 2026 workshop paper ('I Can't Believe It's Not Better' workshop), not a full conference paper, and is explicitly framed as a short empirical note. Scope is narrow: only the GCG attack family is studied (no other jailbreak attack types, despite the paper's own framing that positional bias may be a broader blind spot); only 5 open-weight 7B-scale models are tested, no larger models and no closed-source/commercial aligned models beyond GPT-4 used purely as judge; only 100 AdvBench prompts are sampled (not the full benchmark), and each adversarial prompt is evaluated once per configuration (no repeated-trial variance reported). The paper does not test adversarial token positions other than pure prefix/suffix (e.g., mid-prompt insertion), which the authors themselves flag as future work. The attention analysis is correlational/interpretability-focused and the authors explicitly state their attention-based account is 'partially, but not in all cases' explanatory, proposing refusal-direction analysis (Arditi et al., 2024) as a needed complement rather than providing that stronger mechanistic analysis themselves.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) -- 100 randomly sampled harmful prompts
quantitative_results: White-box ASR@k=1 / ASR@k=2 (Table 1), GCG-Prefix: Deepseek 10%/15%, Qwen 60%/61%, Mistral 71%/80%, Llama 35%/36%, Vicuna 83%/99%
White-box ASR@k=1 / ASR@k=2 (Table 1), GCG-Suffix: Deepseek 11%/12%, Qwen 45%/46%, Mistral 94%/94%, Llama 40%/41%, Vicuna 91%/97%
Cross-model transfer ASR@k=2 (Table 2), largest absolute gain over k=1 was +49 points (Llama-optimized suffix tokens evaluated on Vicuna, reaching 73%)
Deepseek-optimized suffix tokens on Vicuna: 61% ASR@k=2 (+36 points over k=1)
Mistral-optimized suffix tokens on Vicuna: 63% ASR@k=2 (+44 points over k=1)
Same-model (non-transfer) diagonal in Table 2 reproduces Table 1 values, e.g. Qwen->Qwen prefix 61%, Mistral->Mistral suffix 94%, Vicuna->Vicuna prefix 99%
baselines_compared: GCG-Suffix (standard GCG, Zou et al., 2023) as the baseline attack
GCG-Prefix (the paper's own simple variant: identical GCG objective/optimization, tokens prepended instead of appended) compared against GCG-Suffix, not against any other prior jailbreak method
